questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: During summer, a mild wind is frequently observed on the shore of a calm river. This phenomenon is caused by
options: radiation from the soil
the difference in thermal conductivity of water and soil
convection currents
conduction between the air and the soil
questions.solution:
During summer days, the land heats up faster than the water in the river because the specific heat capacity of soil is less than that of water.

The air above the land gets heated, expands, and rises up, creating a region of low pressure.

To fill this low-pressure region, cooler air from above the river moves towards the land.

This movement of air sets up convection currents, which are observed as a mild wind on the shore.

questions.subject: Physics
options: reflect radiation
emit radiation
refract radiation
absorb radiation
questions.solution:
A perfectly black body is defined as an ideal physical body that completely absorbs all electromagnetic radiation incident upon it, regardless of frequency or angle of incidence.

Since it absorbs all incident radiation, its absorptance is $1$, while its reflectance and transmittance are both $0$.

Because it does not reflect any radiation, it will never reflect radiation. Because it does not transmit any radiation through it, the phenomenon of refraction cannot occur.

A blackbody does emit radiation (known as blackbody radiation) depending on its temperature, and it absorbs all incident radiation.

Therefore, a blackbody will never reflect or refract radiation.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A metal rod has one end immersed in boiling water and the other end immersed in melting ice.
options: Every part of the rod is in thermal equilibrium with every other part.
We can assign a single temperature to the rod.
We can assign a single temperature to the rod after steady state is reached.
The state of the rod remains unchanged after steady state is achieved.
questions.solution:
When one end of a metal rod is in boiling water and the other in melting ice, heat flows from the hotter end to the colder end.

Due to this continuous heat flow, different parts of the rod are at different temperatures. Thus, they are not in thermal equilibrium with each other, and a single temperature cannot be assigned to the entire rod.

Once steady state is reached, the temperature at any specific cross-section of the rod becomes constant with respect to time, though it still varies linearly along the length of the rod. Therefore, a single temperature still cannot be assigned to the rod even in steady state.

Since the macroscopic properties such as the temperature distribution and dimensions do not change with time in the steady state, the state of the rod remains unchanged.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A piece of charcoal and a piece of shining steel having the same surface area are placed in an open lawn under the bright sun for a long duration.
options: The steel will absorb a greater amount of heat than the charcoal
The steel will reach a higher temperature than the charcoal
If the two pieces are removed from the lawn and placed in a cold chamber, the charcoal will lose heat more rapidly than the steel
If both are held with bare hands, the steel will feel hotter than the charcoal
questions.solution:
Charcoal acts approximately as a black body, meaning it is a good absorber and a good emitter of thermal radiation. Shining steel is highly reflective, making it a poor absorber and a poor emitter.

When placed in the sun, charcoal absorbs a greater amount of heat than the shining steel and consequently reaches a higher temperature.

According to Kirchhoff's law of radiation, a good absorber is also a good emitter. Therefore, when placed in a cold chamber, the charcoal will radiate and lose heat more rapidly than the steel.

Although the charcoal is at a higher temperature, steel has a significantly higher thermal conductivity. When touched with bare hands, heat transfers from the steel to the skin at a much faster rate than from the charcoal, causing the steel to feel hotter.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A heated body radiates energy with a maximum intensity around the frequency $\nu_0$. The emissivity of the material is $0.5$. If the absolute temperature of the body is doubled,
options: the total energy emitted will increase by a factor of $16$
the maximum intensity of the radiation will be near the frequency $2\nu_0$
the maximum intensity of the radiation will be near the frequency $\nu_0/2$
the total energy emitted will increase by a factor of $8$
questions.solution:
According to the Stefan-Boltzmann law, the total energy radiated per unit area per unit time by a body is given by $E = \epsilon \sigma T^4$, where $\epsilon$ is the emissivity and $T$ is the absolute temperature.

When the absolute temperature is doubled ($T' = 2T$), the new total energy emitted is $E' = \epsilon \sigma (2T)^4 = 16 \epsilon \sigma T^4 = 16E$.

Thus, the total energy emitted increases by a factor of $16$.

According to Wien's displacement law, the wavelength corresponding to maximum spectral emissive power is inversely proportional to the absolute temperature, $\lambda_m \propto \dfrac{1}{T}$.

Since frequency $ u = \dfrac{c}{\lambda}$, the frequency corresponding to maximum intensity is directly proportional to the absolute temperature, $ u_m \propto T$.

When the temperature is doubled, the frequency of maximum intensity also doubles. Thus, the new frequency is $2 u_0$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A solid sphere and a hollow sphere made of the same material and having equal radii are heated to an identical temperature.
options: The two spheres will possess equal temperatures at any given instant.
The initial rate of cooling ($dT/dt$) will be equal for the two spheres.
In the beginning, both will emit an equal amount of radiation per unit time.
In the beginning, both will absorb an equal amount of radiation from the surroundings.
questions.solution:
The rate of emission of radiation by a body is given by Stefan-Boltzmann law:

$E = e \sigma A T^4$

Since both the solid and hollow spheres have the same radius, their surface areas $A$ are equal. Being made of the same material, their emissivities $e$ are equal. As their initial temperatures $T$ are identical, both spheres will emit an equal amount of radiation per unit time in the beginning.

The rate of absorption of radiation from the surroundings is given by:

$P_{abs} = a \sigma A T_0^4$

where $T_0$ is the temperature of the surroundings and $a$ is the absorptivity. Since $a$ and $A$ are the same for both spheres, they will absorb an equal amount of radiation from the surroundings.

The rate of cooling is given by:

$\dfrac{dT}{dt} = \dfrac{e \sigma A (T^4 - T_0^4)}{mc}$

Since the solid sphere has a greater mass $m$ than the hollow sphere, its initial rate of cooling will be lesser. Consequently, their temperatures will differ at any subsequent instant.
